explanation.fileSummary: Chunker.ts is the brain behind splitting a VS Code document into labeled code chunks that an AI or analysis tool can reason about. It first tries to use VS Code's own language intelligence (the document symbol provider) to find meaningful boundaries like functions, classes, and methods. If that fails or returns nothing, it falls back to a simpler line-count-based splitter. When symbols are found, it normalizes and merges overlapping ranges, fills in any uncovered lines with generic 'section' ranges, and then slices every range into pages no taller than a configured maximum, producing a flat list of CodeChunk objects each carrying an ID, line boundaries, a kind label, a dotted symbol path, and the numbered source text.
lines: The file opens by pulling in three collaborators: the vscode API for talking to
the editor, the CodeChunk type that describes what a finished chunk looks like,
and three utilities from the fallback chunker — a plain-text splitter, a

line-numbering formatter, and a line-splitting helper. Imports the entire VS Code extension API, giving access to TextDocument, DocumentSymbol, commands, and more. Imports the CodeChunk interface, which is the shared shape that every produced chunk must conform to. Imports three helpers from the fallback chunker: fallbackChunkText splits raw text when no symbols exist, lineNumberedCode formats a slice of lines with numbers, and splitLines converts a document string into an array of individual lines.
ChunkRange is a small private contract used only inside this file. It records
where a symbol lives in the document (start and end line numbers, both 1-based),
what kind of symbol it is (like 'Function' or 'Class'), and a dotted path
showing its nesting (like 'MyClass.myMethod'). This type is the internal
currency passed between the helper functions before the final CodeChunk shape is

produced. Declares a local TypeScript type called ChunkRange — a plain object shape, not exported, used only within this module. startLine: the 1-based line where this symbol begins in the document. endLine: the 1-based line where this symbol ends. kind: a human-readable label for the symbol category, e.g. 'Function', 'Class', or the fallback 'section'. symbolPath: a dot-joined string showing the full ancestry of the symbol, e.g. 'MyClass.render'.
buildChunks is the public entry point. Its job is to accept a VS Code document
and a line-budget per chunk, then return a promise that resolves to the finished
list of CodeChunk objects. It first asks VS Code for symbol ranges, then splits
the document text into lines. The very first thing it checks is whether any
symbols came back — if not, it immediately hands off to the plain-text fallback,
which knows nothing about code structure but can still produce reasonably sized
pages. Exports an async function — 'async' means it returns a Promise

automatically, allowing 'await' inside. It accepts a TextDocument (the open file) and maxChunkLines (the page-size limit). Awaits the symbol discovery step; the result is an array of ChunkRange objects or an empty array if discovery failed. Converts the full document text into an array of plain strings, one per line, for later use when building code excerpts. Guards against the case where VS Code returned no symbols — this happens for unsupported languages or files with no parseable structure. Falls back to the dumb text chunker, passing the raw text, the language ID, and the max-lines budget, and immediately returns its result.
When symbols were found, two cleanup steps run before slicing. normalizeRanges
clamps line numbers to valid bounds and merges any overlapping symbol regions.

fillGaps then inserts placeholder 'section' ranges for any lines that no symbol claimed. Finally, flatMap visits every range and calls splitRange, which pages it into one or more chunks, and the flattened list becomes the function's return value. Chains normalizeRanges (sort, clamp, merge overlaps) into fillGaps (insert gap-filling sections), producing a gapless, sorted list of ChunkRange objects covering every line. flatMap applies splitRange to each range and flattens the resulting arrays of CodeChunk into a single list, which is returned as the function's result.
getDocumentSymbolRanges is an async helper that asks VS Code's language
intelligence layer for all symbols in the document. It wraps everything in a try
block because the command can fail if no language extension is installed or if
the file has syntax errors. The command 'vscode.executeDocumentSymbolProvider'
is a built-in VS Code command that returns either an array of rich
DocumentSymbol objects (which carry children) or flat SymbolInformation objects,
depending on which extension handles the language. Calls

vscode.commands.executeCommand with a generic type parameter so TypeScript knows the result is an array holding either DocumentSymbol or SymbolInformation objects. The command name 'vscode.executeDocumentSymbolProvider' is a stable VS Code API that invokes whatever language server or extension is registered for the current file type. Passes the document's URI so VS Code knows which file to analyze. The optional-chaining '?.' guards against symbols being null or undefined; if the array is empty or absent, falls into the if block.
If no symbols were found the function returns an empty array, signaling to
buildChunks that it should use the fallback path. Otherwise, flatMap visits
every top-level symbol and calls symbolToRanges, which recursively expands
children. The catch block silently swallows any error (such as a timeout or a
missing provider) and also returns an empty array, ensuring the calling code
always gets a safe value and never crashes. Returns an empty array when symbols
is nullish or empty, triggering the fallback in buildChunks. flatMap expands

each top-level symbol into potentially many ChunkRange entries by recursing into nested children via symbolToRanges. The catch block with no binding (TypeScript 4+ syntax) absorbs any runtime exception and returns an empty array, keeping the overall flow safe.
symbolToRanges converts a single VS Code symbol into one or more ChunkRange
entries and handles both symbol shapes the API can produce. DocumentSymbol has a
selectionRange and a children array, making it a tree node; SymbolInformation is
a flat leaf with a location. The function uses an 'in' check to distinguish them
at runtime, which is TypeScript's type narrowing by property presence. The
parents array carries the ancestor names so the full dotted path can be
assembled. Only top-level symbols (parents is empty) create their own range

entry; nested ones only contribute their children recursively. 'selectionRange' and 'children' are properties unique to DocumentSymbol; checking for them with 'in' narrows the union type so TypeScript allows accessing those fields safely. Uses the symbol's name or falls back to the literal string 'symbol' if the name is empty or falsy. Spread-copies parents and appends the current name, building the ancestry list for children without mutating the original array. topLevel is true when parents is empty, meaning this symbol was returned directly at the root level by the symbol provider. Only top-level symbols produce their own ChunkRange; nested children are skipped at this level to avoid double-counting lines covered by a parent.
For a top-level DocumentSymbol, the range object is built using the symbol's
full range (not just the name selection), converting from 0-based VS Code lines
to 1-based lines by adding 1. SymbolKind is a numeric enum in VS Code; indexing
it by its own value (SymbolKind[symbol.kind]) reverses it to the string name
like 'Function' or 'Class'. Then all children are recursively expanded with the
updated currentPath, and the results are spread together with current into the
return array. symbol.range.start.line is 0-based in VS Code; +1 converts it to

the 1-based convention used throughout this file. vscode.SymbolKind is a numeric enum, so SymbolKind[symbol.kind] performs a reverse lookup to get the string label; '??' provides 'symbol' as a fallback if the lookup fails. Joins the ancestry path with dots to form a readable identifier like 'MyClass.render'. Spreads current (zero or one entry) and then recursively expands every child, returning a flat array of all descendant ranges.
When the symbol does not have selectionRange and children, it must be a flat
SymbolInformation. The code casts it explicitly so TypeScript allows accessing
info.location.range. The name fallback and line conversion (+1) work exactly the
same as in the DocumentSymbol branch. Casts the union type to SymbolInformation
after the type-narrowing 'if' proved it is not a DocumentSymbol.
info.location.range.start.line is also 0-based; +1 brings it in line with the
1-based convention.
The SymbolInformation branch completes its single-element array with the same
kind reverse-lookup and a dotted path assembled from parents plus the current
name, then closes the function. Same SymbolKind reverse-lookup pattern as the
DocumentSymbol branch, keeping the kind label consistent across both shapes.
Builds the symbolPath inline without a variable because SymbolInformation has no children to recurse into.
normalizeRanges takes the raw list of ChunkRange objects that came from symbol
discovery and makes them safe and orderly. The problem it solves is that VS Code
language servers may return line numbers outside the document bounds, ranges
where the end is before the start, or ranges that overlap because a parent and
child both appear in the flat list. The first step maps every range through the
clamp helper to pin start and end within [1, lineCount], then filters out any
range that became invalid (end before start), and finally sorts by start line

ascending, using end line descending as a tiebreaker so that wider ranges come first. Begins a fluent chain: map first, then filter, then sort, storing the sorted result in 'sorted'. Clamps startLine so it can never be less than 1 or greater than lineCount. Same clamping for endLine, guarding against off-by-one errors from different language servers. Removes any range where clamping made startLine greater than endLine, which would be an empty or inverted range.
After sorting, a single pass merges overlapping ranges. The algorithm maintains
a result list and walks sorted ranges one by one. If the current range starts
after the previous range's end, there is no overlap and it is pushed as a new
entry. The continue statement skips the merge logic below, making the
non-overlapping case fast and clear. Sort comparator: primary key is startLine
ascending; secondary key is endLine descending (b minus a) so wider ranges win
ties and get processed first. Iterates over every clamped and validated range in

sorted order. Peeks at the last item in result to check for overlap with the current range. No previous entry yet, or the current range starts strictly after the previous one ends — no overlap, safe to add as a standalone entry. Pushes the non-overlapping range and skips to the next iteration.
When a range does overlap with the previous one, the merge strategy is simple:
extend the previous range's endLine if the current range reaches further.
Because the sort put wider ranges first among ties, this correctly absorbs
nested symbols without producing duplicate coverage. After the loop, the
deduplicated, merged list is returned. Only extends if the current range's end
is beyond the previous entry's end — partial overlaps are absorbed, full
containment is ignored. Mutates previous.endLine in place because previous is a
reference to the last object in result, not a copy. Returns the deduplicated result array to the caller.
fillGaps ensures that every line in the document belongs to exactly one range by
inserting synthetic 'section' ranges wherever normalized symbol ranges leave
gaps. It walks through the ordered symbol ranges with a cursor that starts at
line 1. Whenever the next symbol range starts after the cursor, the lines in
between are unclaimed and get a gap-filler range with kind 'section' and
symbolPath 'top-level'. Accepts the already-normalized, gap-free (but not
gap-filled) range list and the total line count of the document. cursor tracks

the next line that has not yet been assigned to any range; starts at 1 because lines are 1-based. Iterates over every symbol range in order. If cursor has not yet reached this range's start, there is an unclaimed gap between cursor and the range's first line. Begins building the gap-filler ChunkRange object starting at cursor.
The gap-filler range ends just before the symbol range begins, giving it the
kind 'section' and the path 'top-level' to indicate it is not part of any named
symbol. After inserting the gap (if any), the actual symbol range is pushed into
the output and cursor advances to the line immediately after the symbol range
ends, ready to detect the next gap. Ends the gap one line before the symbol
range starts, so the two ranges are perfectly adjacent with no overlap. Labels
gap ranges as 'section' to distinguish them from symbol-derived ranges. Pushes
the actual symbol range after any gap range, preserving its kind and symbolPath. Advances cursor to the line after this symbol range so the next iteration can detect any subsequent gap.
After the loop, there may still be lines at the end of the file that no symbol
covered — the trailing gap. If cursor has not yet reached lineCount, a final
section range is appended that runs from cursor to the last line of the
document. Checks whether cursor still has unprocessed lines remaining after the
last symbol range. Pushes a trailing section range to cover every line from
cursor to the document's end. startLine is cursor, the first line after the last
symbol range ended. endLine is lineCount, the total number of lines, making this
range reach the very last line.
The function closes the trailing section object and returns filled, which now
contains an unbroken sequence of ChunkRange objects that together cover every
line from 1 to lineCount with no gaps and no overlaps. Returns the complete,
gap-free list of ChunkRange objects ready for splitRange to page into CodeChunk
objects.
splitRange is the final transformation step. Its job is to take one ChunkRange —
which might span hundreds of lines — and slice it into one or more CodeChunk
objects, each no taller than maxChunkLines. Think of it like paginating a long
chapter of a book: each page gets the same chapter title plus a part number if
it spills beyond the first page. A while loop advances startLine by
maxChunkLines each iteration until every line in the range has been assigned to
a chunk. Accepts the range to slice, the full array of document lines for

extracting text, the line budget per chunk, and the range's index in the parent array (used to build stable IDs). startLine moves forward each iteration; begins at the range's own startLine. part is a 1-based counter that becomes the second segment of the chunk ID and is appended to the symbolPath for parts beyond the first. Loop continues as long as there are lines left in the range to assign. Calculates the end of this page: either maxChunkLines away from startLine or the range's own endLine, whichever comes first.
Each chunk is given a stable composite ID like 'chunk-3-2' (range index 3, part
2). The symbolPath stays clean for the first part but gets ' part N' appended
for subsequent pages so the reader can tell that two chunks belong to the same
symbol. lineNumberedCode formats the slice of lines with inline line numbers so
the AI or viewer always knows where in the file each line lives. After pushing,
startLine jumps to the line after this page ends. ID uses template literals:
rangeIndex+1 is 1-based to match the human-readable chunk numbering, and part

starts at 1. First part keeps the clean symbolPath; subsequent parts append ' part N' so long functions remain traceable across pages. lineNumberedCode slices lines[startLine-1 .. endLine-1] and prepends each line's number, producing the code field of the chunk. Advances startLine to begin the next page immediately after this one, ensuring no lines are skipped or duplicated.
The part counter increments each iteration so subsequent pages get correct
labels, and the loop ends when startLine has passed range.endLine. The function
returns the chunks array, which may contain just one element for short ranges or
many for very long ones. Increments part so the next iteration produces 'part
2', 'part 3', etc. in the symbolPath and ID. Returns all produced CodeChunk objects for this range to the flatMap call in buildChunks.
clamp is a tiny pure utility that constrains a number to a closed interval [min,
max]. It is used by normalizeRanges to prevent line numbers from going below 1

or above the document's line count. The implementation nests Math.min inside Math.max: first min(max, value) caps the top, then max(min, ...) raises the bottom. Accepts any number and the inclusive lower and upper bounds. Math.min(max, value) prevents the value from exceeding max; wrapping with Math.max(min, ...) then prevents it from going below min.
